---
title: "R + Python in Quarto"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    code-fold: true
jupyter: python3
---

## Setup

Quarto handles both Python and R chunks in the same document.
Data flows between them via `reticulate`.

```{python}
#| label: py-setup
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import seaborn as sns
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# Make some data in Python
rng = np.random.default_rng(42)
n = 200

df = pd.DataFrame({
    "x": rng.normal(0, 1, n),
    "y": rng.normal(0, 1, n),
    "group": rng.choice(["A", "B", "C"], n)
})

print(df.head())
```

```{python}
#| label: py-plot
#| fig-cap: "Seaborn scatterplot — done in Python"
sns.scatterplot(data=df, x="x", y="y", hue="group", alpha=0.7)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

## Handing off to R

Use `reticulate::py$` to access any Python object in R.

```{r}
#| label: r-setup
library(ggplot2)
library(dplyr)

# Grab the dataframe created in the Python chunk above
df_r <- reticulate::py$df

glimpse(df_r)
```

```{r}
#| label: r-plot
#| fig-cap: "ggplot2 — same data, rendered in R"
ggplot(df_r, aes(x = x, y = y, colour = group)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.7) +
  facet_wrap(~group) +
  theme_minimal()
```

## Passing data back to Python

```{r}
#| label: r-model
# Run a model in R, pass summary stats back to Python
model_summary <- df_r |>
  group_by(group) |>
  summarise(
    mean_x = mean(x),
    mean_y = mean(y),
    cor_xy = cor(x, y)
  )

# This becomes available as py$r.model_summary in subsequent Python chunks
```

```{python}
#| label: py-use-r-output
import r  # reticulate magic — access R objects via the `r` module

summary_df = r.model_summary
print(summary_df)
```
